en.extract:
The large-tailed nightjar (Caprimulgus macrurus) is a species of nightjar known for its distinctive long, broad tail and cryptic plumage that allows it to blend into its surroundings. Found along the southern Himalayan foothills, eastern South Asia, Southeast Asia and northern Australia, its natural habitats are subtropical or tropical moist lowland forest, subtropical or tropical mangrove forest, and subtropical or tropical moist montane forest.


Description
The large-tailed nightjar measures approximately 31.5 to 33 cm in length. The cryptic plumage and colouration of large-tailed nightjars provide effective camouflage within its natural habitat.
Males exhibit distinctive markings, characterized by a pale crown with a dark median stripe. A prominent row of black scapular feathers, bordered by broad buff or whitish-buff fringes, enhances their striking appearance. Additionally, males possess a white throat patch and along, broad brown tail marked with uneven dark bars. Large white patches on its primaries and outer tail feathers can be observed in flight.
In contrast, females display a paler and greyer plumage compared to males. They have smaller buff-coloured wing patches and less vibrant tail patches, resulting in a drabber appearance.


Taxonomy
The large-tailed nightjar (Caprimulgus macrurus) is a species of nightjar belonging to the family Caprimulgidae. There are 6 subspecies presently recognized (C.m. albonotatus, C.m. bimaculatus, C.m. macrurus, C.m. salvadorii, C.m. johnsoni, C.m. schlegelii). Originally a species complex of 12 different subspecies, differences in territorial vocalizations have been interpreted as evidence for reproductive isolation and, consequently, the delegation of some members of the complex as new species of nightjar under the biological species concept.


Habitat and distribution
The large-tailed nightjar is found across a diverse range of habitats within subtropical and tropical zones. This species is a resident of the countries of Australia, Bangladesh, Bhutan, Brunei, Cambodia, China, India, Indonesia, Laos, Malaysia, Myanmar, Nepal, Pakistan, Papua New Guinea, Philippines, Singapore, Thailand, Timor-Leste and Vietnam. Its distribution spans a wide geographical area, extending from northern India through eastern China and Indonesia to northern Australia.
This species commonly inhabits secondary forests, forest edges, plantations, and wooded parks. It has also been observed in agricultural landscapes, where its presence may be of significant economic interest to farmers, as large-tailed nightjars are believed to play a role in the biological control of insect pests.


Behaviour
The large-tailed nightjar's remarkable camouflage and nocturnal lifestyle pose significant challenges for studying their behaviour and ecology. Similar to other nightjar species, they depend primarily on acoustic communication rather than visual cues for inter-specific and intra-specific communication.


Vocalizations
Songs can be heard year-round, but vocal activity may vary depending on breeding activity, population density, and moon phase. The large-tailed nightjar has a distinctive, monotonous song that it uses to assert its territory. Territorial songs are typically given by males, but there are also reports of females giving these songs. Singing is most notable during courtship and early breeding, primarily taking place from perches or occasionally in pursuit-flights.
Long-tailed nightjars also perform grunting calls, which are very different from territorial songs. They are best described as a series of deep, low-pitched "wroww" calls or a series of fast-repeated "wrrrog" or "grrrog" notes.


Diet
Large-tailed nightjars are aerial insectivores. Their diets are primarily composed of beetles, moths, and other flying insects consumed while the bird is in flight. However, there have been a few observations of the large-tailed nightjar walking and pecking at the ground, apparently preying on terrestrial ants. It has been suggested that, while ground foraging is not common, it may occur more frequently than previously thought, as a response to low abundance of flying insects.


Reproduction
Little is known about the large-tailed nightjar's nesting and roosting behaviour. The breeding season most likely extends from mid-March to late September. The female does not build a nest and instead lays 1-2 eggs in a shallow depression of leaf litter on the ground. The eggs are pinkish-buff to pale yellow in colour and chicks have mealy brown feathers that ensure good camouflage in their nest site. Nightjar chicks emerge with downy feathers and may be moved from the nest site 1–2 days after hatching. Chicks move every night and siblings will sometimes separate, likely to avoid detection by predators.


References


External links

The '''large-tailed nightjar''' ('''''Caprimulgus macrurus''''') is a species of [[nightjar]] known for its distinctive long, broad tail and cryptic plumage that allows it to blend into its surroundings. Found along the southern [[Himalayas|Himalayan]] foothills, eastern [[South Asia]], [[Southeast Asia]] and northern [[Australia]], its natural [[habitat]]s are [[subtropical or tropical moist lowland forest]], subtropical or tropical [[mangrove forest]], and subtropical or tropical moist [[montane forest]].<ref name=":4" />

== Description ==
The large-tailed nightjar measures approximately 31.5 to 33 cm in length.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book |last=Robson |first=Craig |title=Field Guide to the Birds of South-East Asia |publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing |year=2014 |isbn=978-1-4729-7040-4 |edition=2nd |location=London}}</ref> The cryptic [[plumage]] and colouration of large-tailed nightjars provide effective [[camouflage]] within its natural habitat.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last1=Sangster |first1=George |last2=Rozendaal |first2=Frank |title=Systematic notes on Asian birds. 41. Territorial songs and species-level taxonomy of nightjars of the Caprimulgus macrurus complex, with the description of a new species |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/228462801 |journal=Zoologische Verhandelingen |volume=350}}</ref>

Males exhibit distinctive markings, characterized by a pale crown with a dark median stripe. A prominent row of black [[scapular feathers]], bordered by broad buff or whitish-buff fringes, enhances their striking appearance.<ref name=":0" /> Additionally, males possess a white throat patch and along, broad brown tail marked with uneven dark bars.<ref name=":2">{{Cite journal |last=Nath |first=Anukul |date=January 2014 |title=Observations of the Nesting of Large-tailed Nightjar Caprimulgus macrurus |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/277331306 |journal=BirdingAsia |pages=94–95}}</ref> Large white patches on its [[Flight feather|primaries]] and outer tail feathers can be observed in flight.

In contrast, females display a paler and greyer plumage compared to males. They have smaller buff-coloured wing patches and less vibrant tail patches, resulting in a drabber appearance.<ref name=":0" />

== Taxonomy ==
The large-tailed nightjar (''Caprimulgus macrurus'') is a species of nightjar belonging to the family [[Nightjar|Caprimulgidae]].<ref>{{Cite web |last= |first= |date= |title=Large-tailed Nightjar Caprimulgus macrurus Horsfield, T 1821 |url=https://avibase.bsc-eoc.org/species.jsp?avibaseid=04DDADF0515EECDA |access-date=October 16, 2024 |website=Avibase - The World Bird Database}}</ref> There are 6 [[subspecies]] presently recognized (''C.m. albonotatus, C.m. bimaculatus, C.m. macrurus, C.m. salvadorii, C.m. johnsoni, C.m. schlegelii'').<ref name=":0" /> Originally a [[species complex]] of 12 different subspecies, differences in territorial [[Bird vocalization|vocalizations]] have been interpreted as evidence for [[reproductive isolation]] and, consequently, the delegation of some members of the complex as new species of nightjar under the [[Species|biological species concept]].<ref name=":1" />

== Habitat and distribution ==
The large-tailed nightjar is found across a diverse range of habitats within [[Subtropics|subtropical]] and [[Tropics|tropical zones]]. This species is a resident of the countries of [[Australia]], [[Bangladesh]], [[Bhutan]], [[Brunei]], [[Cambodia]], [[China]], [[India]], [[Indonesia]], [[Laos]], [[Malaysia]], [[Myanmar]], [[Nepal]], [[Pakistan]], [[Papua New Guinea]], [[Philippines]], [[Singapore]], [[Thailand]], [[Timor-Leste]] and [[Vietnam]].<ref name="iucn status 15 November 2021" /><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Oberholser |first=Harry C. |date=1915 |title=A synopsis of the races of the long-tailed goatsucker, Caprimulgus macrurus Horsfield |journal=Proceedings of the United States National Museum |volume=48 |issue=2088 |pages=587–599 |doi=10.5479/si.00963801.48-2088.587 |issn=0096-3801}}</ref><ref name=":4">{{cite journal |last1=Mees |first1=G. F. |year=1977 |title=Geographical variation of Caprimulgus macrurus Horsfield (aves, caprimulgidae) |url=https://repository.naturalis.nl/pub/317588 |journal=Zoologische Verhandelingen |location=Leiden |publisher=Brill |volume=155 |issue=1 |pages=1–47 |lccn=78307720}}</ref> Its distribution spans a wide geographical area, extending from [[North India|northern India]] through [[East China|eastern China]] and [[Indonesia]] to northern [[Australia]].<ref name=":2" />

This species commonly inhabits [[secondary forest]]s, [[Woodland edge|forest edges]], [[plantation]]s, and wooded parks.<ref name=":0" /> It has also been observed in agricultural landscapes, where its presence may be of significant economic interest to farmers, as large-tailed nightjars are believed to play a role in the [[Biological pest control|biological control]] of insect pests.<ref name=":3">{{Cite journal |last1=Chang |first1=Phooi Kuan |last2=Puan |first2=Chong Leong |last3=Yee |first3=Siew Ann |last4=Abu |first4=Jalila |date=2018-04-03 |title=Vocal individuality of large-tailed nightjar ( Caprimulgus macrurus ) in Peninsular Malaysia |url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/09524622.2017.1292408 |journal=Bioacoustics |language=en |volume=27 |issue=2 |pages=131–144 |doi=10.1080/09524622.2017.1292408 |bibcode=2018Bioac..27..131C |issn=0952-4622|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

== Behaviour ==
The large-tailed nightjar's remarkable camouflage and [[Nocturnality|nocturnal]] lifestyle pose significant challenges for studying their behaviour and [[ecology]]. Similar to other nightjar species, they depend primarily on [[Bird vocalization|acoustic communication]] rather than visual cues for [[Interspecies communication|inter-specific]] and [[Intraspecific communication|intra-specific]] communication.<ref name=":3" />

=== Vocalizations ===
Songs can be heard year-round, but vocal activity may vary depending on breeding activity, [[population density]], and [[Lunar phase|moon phase]]. The large-tailed nightjar has a distinctive, monotonous song that it uses to assert its territory.<ref name=":3" /> Territorial songs are typically given by males, but there are also reports of females giving these songs. Singing is most notable during [[Courtship display|courtship]] and early breeding, primarily taking place from perches or occasionally in pursuit-flights.<ref name=":1" />

Long-tailed nightjars also perform grunting calls, which are very different from territorial songs. They are best described as a series of deep, low-pitched "''wroww''" calls or a series of fast-repeated "''wrrrog''" or "''grrrog''" notes.<ref name=":1" />

=== Diet ===
Large-tailed nightjars are [[Hawking (birds)|aerial insectivores]]. Their diets are primarily composed of [[beetle]]s, [[moth]]s, and other [[Pterygota|flying insects]] consumed while the bird is in flight.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Schodde |first1=Richard |title=Reader's Digest Complete Book of Australian Birds |last2=Tidemann |first2=Sonia |publisher=Reader's Digest Services ty Ltd |year=1986 |isbn=0-949819-99-9 |location=Sydney}}</ref> However, there have been a few observations of the large-tailed nightjar walking and pecking at the ground, apparently preying on [[Terrestrial ecosystem|terrestrial]] [[ant]]s. It has been suggested that, while ground [[foraging]] is not common, it may occur more frequently than previously thought, as a response to low abundance of flying insects.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=James |first=David |date=December 2000 |title=Large-tailed Nightjars Caprimulgus macrurus Apparently Taking Ants From the Ground |journal=The Sunbird - Journal of the Queensland Ornithological Society |volume=30 |issue=3 |pages=89–91}}</ref>

=== Reproduction ===
Little is known about the large-tailed nightjar's nesting and roosting behaviour. The [[Seasonal breeder|breeding season]] most likely extends from mid-March to late September.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Lamont |first=Angus R. |date=2000 |title=Breeding habits and behaviour of Large-tailed Nightjar Caprimulgus macrurus in Singapore |url= |journal=Forktail |volume=16 |pages=166–167}}</ref> The female does not build a nest and instead lays 1-2 eggs in a shallow depression of [[Plant litter|leaf litter]] on the ground.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Wells |first=David R. |title=The Birds of the Thai-Malay Peninsula: Vol. 1 - Non-passerines |publisher=Princeton University Press |year=1999 |isbn=978-0-12-742961-8 |edition=1}}</ref> The eggs are pinkish-buff to pale yellow in colour and chicks have mealy brown feathers that ensure good camouflage in their nest site. Nightjar chicks emerge with [[Down feather|downy feathers]] and may be moved from the nest site 1–2 days after hatching. Chicks move every night and siblings will sometimes separate, likely to avoid detection by [[Predation|predators]].<ref name=":2" />
